Both systems succeeded in suggesting existing drugs that could target diseases—acute myeloid leukemia and macular degeneration—by finding non-obvious connections across disparate fields of research. The work, published in Nature, demonstrates a practical application of AI where the constraint is not performance against humans but rather scale: systematically reviewing the volume of scientific information that no single researcher could reasonably cover.

## How the Systems Operate

Google's Co-Scientist, built on the company's Gemini language model, interprets researcher-defined research goals and searches the literature to form hypotheses. These hypotheses compete in a "tournament" format, evaluated by a Reflection agent that has access to external search tools to prevent hallucinations. An Evolution agent refines surviving ideas through additional iterations. Scientists remain in the loop throughout, with expert panels reviewing and prioritizing suggestions before validation.

FutureHouse's Robin takes a similar agentic approach but with specialized tools tailored to literature synthesis. Crow produces concise paper summaries while Falcon provides detailed analysis. The efficiency gains are stark: Robin analyzed 551 papers in 30 minutes, compared to an estimated 540 hours for human review. Robin then generated disease mechanism hypotheses and ranked them through pairwise comparisons using an LLM judge.

## Why Literature Synthesis Matters Now

The explosion of online publishing has made it nearly impossible for individual researchers to stay current within their own field, let alone identify relevant findings elsewhere. A researcher studying eye development might miss critical insights from kidney research that involve the same signaling systems. These systems address what FutureHouse calls "combinatorial synthesis"—finding non-obvious connections across compartmentalized scientific knowledge.

This is fundamentally a problem of scale, not capability replacement. Neither system attempts to design novel drugs or replace scientific judgment. Instead, they handle information processing that humans rarely attempt due to sheer volume. The practical value is substantial: by surfacing overlooked connections, these tools can direct researchers toward promising hypotheses worth testing. That makes them genuinely useful infrastructure for scientific work, grounded in the constraint that matters most—researcher time and attention.
items.original_title: Two AI-based science assistants succeed with drug-retargeting tasks

items.headline: OpenAI, Nvidia adopt Google's SynthID watermark for AI-generated content
items.body:
Google's SynthID watermarking technology is moving beyond Google's own systems. OpenAI will embed SynthID in DALL-E 3 images, Nvidia will integrate it into its Cosmos world foundation models, and ElevenLabs and Kakao are also adopting the standard. This expansion signals a shift toward industry-wide tagging of synthetic media, though significant gaps in coverage will persist.

## Adoption Across Major AI Vendors

SynthID embeds imperceptible markers directly into the pixel data of images and videos, and into the waveform of audio—making it resistant to common transformations like cropping, compression, and rotation. Google claims the watermark has been applied to 100 billion images and videos plus 60,000 years of audio over the past three years. The new partnerships extend that reach to some of the industry's largest model providers. Google is also preparing an enterprise API for detecting SynthID-watermarked content, initially available through its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, allowing business partners to programmatically flag synthetic media.

Detection will become more accessible. Users will soon check for SynthID watermarks via Circle to Search, Google Lens, and Chrome-based Gemini sessions without opening a dedicated app. However, Google is restricting public API access to SynthID scanning, citing security concerns—widespread availability of detection tools could help adversaries develop bypasses.

## The Coverage Problem Remains

SynthID adoption by OpenAI and Nvidia matters, but it leaves the AI content landscape largely unaddressed. The vast majority of AI-generated images currently online carry no watermark at all. Open-source models—available for anyone to download and run locally—will continue producing untagged content. Without universal adoption or legal mandates, SynthID effectively creates a two-tier system: marked content from major vendors and unmarked content from everywhere else.

Google also maintains C2PA metadata tagging as a parallel strategy, labeling content with creation details. Both approaches are complementary but insufficient on their own. C2PA metadata is easily stripped; SynthID is harder to remove but only appears in newly generated content from participating vendors.

For enterprises and content platforms, the practical implication is clear: SynthID adoption by major AI labs reduces friction in identifying synthetic media from those specific sources. However, relying on watermarks alone leaves significant blind spots. Organizations managing AI content risk at scale will need multiple detection strategies alongside watermarking—none yet reliable enough to be definitive.
items.original_title: Google's SynthID AI watermarking tech is being adopted by OpenAI, Nvidia, and more
